title: Swin Transformer: Hierarchical Vision Transformer using Shifted Windows
problem: Standard Vision Transformers use fixed-scale tokens and a single low-resolution feature map, which makes them awkward as drop-in backbones for dense tasks such as object detection and semantic segmentation.
Global self-attention over image patches has quadratic cost in the number of patches, so high-resolution images and pixel-level prediction become expensive.
Purely local self-attention can reduce cost, but non-overlapping windows isolate regions from one another, while sliding-window attention has inefficient memory access patterns on general hardware.
solution: Swin Transformer builds a hierarchical feature pyramid: an image is split into small patches, embedded as tokens, and then patch-merging layers repeatedly combine neighboring 2 x 2 tokens to reduce spatial resolution while increasing channel width (5a).

Each Swin block replaces global multi-head self-attention with window multi-head self-attention over fixed M x M windows, changing attention complexity from quadratic in the patch count to linear when the window size is fixed, as shown in **4a**.

Consecutive blocks alternate regular window attention and shifted-window attention, so the shifted block crosses the previous block's window boundaries and creates cross-window communication without global attention (5b, **4b**).

The shifted configuration is implemented efficiently with cyclic shift and an attention mask, preserving the same number of batched windows while preventing tokens from attending across unrelated sub-windows.
Attention inside each window uses a learnable relative position bias **4c**, giving the model a locality-aware geometric signal without relying on absolute position embeddings.
key_concepts: Hierarchical patch merging gives the Transformer a CNN-like multi-scale interface. The stages produce progressively coarser feature maps, making the backbone compatible with feature-pyramid and encoder-decoder designs used for dense prediction.

Window attention is the efficiency lever: because each token attends only within an M x M neighborhood, the attention term scales with the number of windows rather than all pairwise image-token interactions **4a**.

Shifted windows are the modeling lever: regular windows are efficient but disconnected, so alternating with shifted partitions lets information propagate across neighboring windows over successive blocks (5b).

Cyclic shift turns the irregular shifted partition into a regular batched computation. The attention mask restores the intended window boundaries after the shift, avoiding the padding overhead of a naive shifted-window implementation.

Relative position bias preserves useful visual inductive bias inside each local window by making attention depend on pairwise spatial offsets **4c**; the paper reports it as preferable to absolute position embeddings for general-purpose vision backbones.

math.label: window attention with relative position bias

visuals.caption: Hierarchical Swin backbone versus single-scale ViT
visuals.description: Extracted Figure 1 contrasts Swin's patch-merging hierarchy and local-window attention with ViT's single-scale global-attention design.

visuals.caption: Shifted window self-attention
visuals.description: Extracted Figure 2 shows regular window partitioning in one layer and shifted partitioning in the next, illustrating how neighboring windows interact without global attention.
